brand_focus: Cooking tips, recipes, and marinades with AI-styled ingredient photography. Audience is Europe-first, US a strong second. Goal: grow followers fast-but-safe by making every post highly shareable (sent to a friend) and saveable.

slots.note: Tue ~18:23 CET — EU dinner-prep window / US Eastern lunch crossover. Carousel turns the marinade into step-by-step save-bait; ratios on each slide drive saves and sends.
Wed ~12:41 CET — strongest food-search day; carousel recipe format maximises saves from meal-planners.
Fri ~19:07 CET — weekend-cook intent begins Friday evening. Versatile sauce carousel (ratios on screen) is highly sendable and saveable for the weekend ahead.
Sat ~11:17 CET — weekend brunch scroll. A single striking technique visual with a search-friendly caption; simpler format tests image reach vs carousel on this slot.

content_plan.marinade: One complete marinade built for saves: lead with the ratio (e.g. 3-2-1 oil/acid/flavour), name the best proteins, give a food-safe time range, and end with a bold visual of the finished dish. Every slide should contain one piece of information worth screenshotting.
content_plan.quick-dinner: A genuinely fast (~25-30 min) weeknight recipe. Carousel arc: slide 1 = finished dish with bold outcome headline, slide 2 = short ingredient list with quantities visible, slides 3-4 = one key technique step each, final slide = 'save this for tonight' CTA. Keep ingredient count ≤7.
content_plan.sauce: A versatile sauce, dressing, or glaze with a ratio people can memorise (e.g. 2-1-1). Show it on at least two different foods. Final slide lists 3 quick uses — this drives sends ('send this to someone who always asks what to put on things').
content_plan.technique: One practical, slightly surprising kitchen technique — the kind of thing that makes someone say 'I didn't know that.' Single hero image with keyword-rich caption explaining the full technique. Designed to be sent to a friend.

caption_strategy: Write captions as search-first, value-first copy: open with the outcome or the specific dish name (e.g. 'This 3-ingredient honey-soy marinade keeps chicken thighs juicy for up to 48 hours') so the first line reads like a recipe search query and doubles as the preview text. Front-load the most useful information — ratios, times, temperatures — because that is what drives saves. Close every caption with a soft send-prompt ('send this to someone who always overcooks chicken') before any hashtags. Keep captions between 80-180 words; avoid filler phrases like 'hope you enjoy' or 'let me know in the comments.' Use line breaks generously so captions are scannable on mobile.
hashtag_strategy: Use exactly 4-5 hashtags, placed at the end of the caption after a line break. Prioritise mid-size, recipe-specific tags (100k-2M posts) over giant generic ones: e.g. #marinaderecipe, #quickdinnerideas, #cookingforbeginners, #weeknightmeals, #saucegame. Avoid tags above 5M posts (too competitive for a new account) and below 10k (no discovery traffic). Rotate one hashtag per post to gather data on which drives non-follower reach. Never use more than 5; keyword-rich captions matter far more than hashtag volume.
learnings: No performance data exists yet — account is 7 days old with 0 measured posts. Cold-start rules apply: (1) Every post must earn its save — include a concrete ratio, time, or step viewers will want to reference later. (2) Format-shift to carousels on 3 of 4 posts; carousels create multiple scroll interactions and stronger save signals. (3) Write captions as recipe search queries to capture keyword discovery. (4) Every post needs one explicit send-prompt. (5) Hold cadence at 4/week; do not increase until at least 2 posts show positive sends_per_reach. Reassess after first 8 posts.
